key_insight: A prospective ICU AI pilot showed that the biggest challenge was not model performance alone, but how the system fit into workflow, governance, accountability, and learning processes.
clinical_relevance: For hospital teams considering clinical AI, the study suggests early pilots should be designed to generate operational and governance evidence, not just prediction metrics.
method: Prospective, single-center mixed-methods implementation evaluation in a surgical ICU.
Evaluated workflow integration, governance/data protection, live benchmarking, ethics/accountability, and human-AI interaction.
Included 267 consecutive ICU stays and a bedside user-heterogeneity assessment instrument.
evidence_signal: Among 267 ICU stays, consultants used the tool in 72% and residents in 61%; live benchmarking also revealed occasional latency-related inconsistencies in the daily updated case list.
watch_item: Air-gapped governance and once-daily data refreshes improved compliance but introduced operational delay, so real-time usability may be limited in fast-changing settings.
paper_title: Managing AI-Enabled Uncertainty in Clinical AI Deployment: Mixed-Methods Study of Governance, Workflow, and Organizational Learning in an ICU Decision Support Pilot
